journal_entry.description: Major overhaul of machinery extending useful life
journal_entry.rationale: Under IFRS (IAS 16), subsequent expenditure on property, plant and equipment should be capitalized when it extends the useful life or enhances the asset's capacity. The $200,000 overhaul extends the remaining useful life by 2 years beyond the original estimate, meeting the capitalization criteria. The cost is added to the carrying amount of the machinery asset (or can be recorded as a separate component if the entity uses component accounting). Cash is reduced by the payment amount. No tax lines are included as capital expenditures on owned assets are not subject to sales tax recovery in the same transaction (any HST/GST paid would have been included in the stated $200,000 amount).

output.reason: The $200,000 cash payment for the major overhaul represents an asset decrease (cash leaving the business). The overhaul is capitalized (added to the machinery asset account on the debit side) because it extends useful life, but on the credit side we only see the cash outflow. One credit line: cash decrease = asset decrease.

credit_classifier.reason: The $200,000 cash payment for the major overhaul represents an asset decrease (cash leaving the business). The overhaul is capitalized (added to the machinery asset account on the debit side) because it extends useful life, but on the credit side we only see the cash outflow. One credit line: cash decrease = asset decrease.

output.reason: The $200,000 major overhaul extends the useful life of the machinery, which qualifies as a capital improvement under IFRS (IAS 16). This expenditure enhances the future economic benefits of the asset beyond its original standard of performance and should be capitalized (added to the asset's carrying amount), not expensed. Therefore, this is 1 asset increase (debit to Machinery or similar fixed asset account).

debit_classifier.reason: The $200,000 major overhaul extends the useful life of the machinery, which qualifies as a capital improvement under IFRS (IAS 16). This expenditure enhances the future economic benefits of the asset beyond its original standard of performance and should be capitalized (added to the asset's carrying amount), not expensed. Therefore, this is 1 asset increase (debit to Machinery or similar fixed asset account).

output.reason: Entry correctly capitalizes the $200,000 overhaul expenditure to Machinery (debit) and reduces Cash (credit). Under IAS 16, subsequent expenditure that extends useful life meets capitalization criteria. Balance verified: $200,000 debit = $200,000 credit. No tax lines required - capital expenditures on owned PPE are not taxable transactions (any sales tax paid would be embedded in the stated amount). Accounts, amounts, and directionality all correct.
